title: The Neighbor Who Never Left
excerpt: Everyone on the street knew something was wrong with Mr. Kapoor's house. The lights were always on, but no one had seen him leave in months.
content: Everyone on the street knew something was wrong with Mr. Kapoor's house. The lights were always on, but no one had seen him leave in months. The newspapers piled at his gate, the milk bottles untouched every morning. Still, nobody knocked.

It was the girl from across the street — Priya, barely twelve years old — who first noticed the smell. She told her mother, who told her husband, who called the building watchman. The watchman stood at the iron gate for a full ten minutes before he finally called the police.

What the officers found inside wasn't what anyone expected. Mr. Kapoor was very much alive, sitting in his recliner with a cup of tea gone cold. He looked up at them with mild irritation, the way a man looks when he's been interrupted mid-thought.
'I was waiting,' he said simply.
Waiting for what, they asked. He turned to look at the staircase — the one that led to the upper floor no one on the street knew existed — and smiled.
'For her to come back down,' he whispered.

They found no one upstairs. Only footprints in the dust. Fresh ones. Small ones. Leading in circles, over and over, around an empty room. The window was locked from the inside. The door had no keyhole.

Mr. Kapoor refused to leave. They had to carry him out. As they took him through the gate, he kept twisting his neck to look back at that upper window. Priya was watching from her balcony. She saw it too.
A shadow at the glass. Watching them go.
They boarded the house up that same evening. Three weeks later, the boards were found on the inside of the front door. Neatly stacked, as if someone had been tidying up.
The house is still there. The lights still come on every night, at exactly 9 PM. Nobody lives there. Nobody owns it anymore. The municipality records show the plot as empty land.
Priya is thirty now. She doesn't live in that city anymore. But every night, at exactly 9 PM, she finds herself looking at the clock. She's never told anyone why.

title: The WhatsApp Group Nobody Left
excerpt: The group was called 'Old Class Reunion 2014'. Nobody remembered creating it. Nobody remembered joining it either.
content: The group was called 'Old Class Reunion 2014'. Nobody remembered creating it. Nobody remembered joining it either. But there it was in Rohit's phone, sandwiched between a grocery reminder and his mother's chat, with 23 members and a notification badge that read 847.
He scrolled up, expecting old jokes and graduation photos. What he found instead were conversations that had been going on for six years without him.

Not conversations, exactly. More like check-ins. Every few weeks, someone would post a single line: 'Still here.' Others would respond with the same two words, like a roll call. The names were from his batch — Anjali, Deepak, Siddharth, Meera. Friends he hadn't thought about in years.
He typed 'Hey guys, when did you add me?' and went to sleep.
At 3 AM his phone lit up. Not with one reply. With all of them. Every single member of the group, replying within the same minute: 'We didn't add you.'

He called Anjali the next morning. She sounded normal, warm, happy to hear from him. Yes, she was in a WhatsApp group from college, she said. But she hadn't posted in it for ages. Her last message was from 2019.
'What did it say?' he asked.
'Still here,' she said. Then a pause. 'Why?'
Rohit checked his phone. The group was gone. Not archived. Gone. He scrolled through his chats, his archived folders, his deleted messages. Nothing.
But the 847 unread notifications were still in his notification drawer. No app attached to them. No sender. Just the same message, repeated 847 times.
'Still here.'
He switched phones that afternoon. On his new phone, he had one WhatsApp notification waiting. From an unknown number. No profile picture. No name in his contacts.
It said: 'Welcome back.'

title: The Recording That Played Itself
excerpt: The voicemail was from her own number. She recognized her own voice immediately — except she had never made that call.
content: The voicemail was from her own number. Nandita recognized her own voice immediately — the particular softness of it, the way she said certain vowels, the small breath she always took before speaking. Except she had never made that call.

The timestamp said 3:17 AM, last Tuesday. Nandita had been asleep at 3:17 AM. She was certain of this because she remembered her alarm going off at 5:30 and feeling like she'd only just closed her eyes.
She played the voicemail again. Her own voice, calm and deliberate: 'Don't go to the office tomorrow. I'm asking you not to go. Please.'
That was all. Twelve seconds of audio. Her voice, her number, her breathing pattern.
She had gone to the office. Nothing happened. It was an ordinary Wednesday.

She showed the voicemail to her husband. He listened twice and said it did sound like her. She showed it to her sister, who said the same thing. She played it to her own voicemail greeting to compare — the cadence was identical, right down to the pause before 'please'.

She called her carrier. They confirmed the call had originated from her registered number. Her phone showed no record of an outgoing call at that time. Her phone's screen-on log showed it had been asleep since midnight.
She tried to save the voicemail as an audio file. Her phone crashed when she attempted the export. When it restarted, the voicemail was gone — but a new one had replaced it.
Same number. Same voice. Same 3:17 AM timestamp.
'Don't go to the office tomorrow.'
It was Sunday when she found the second voicemail. Monday morning she called in sick.
There was a gas explosion at her office building that afternoon. Two floors collapsed. Her floor — the fourteenth — was the worst affected.
She still has her phone. The second voicemail is still on it. She hasn't played it in three months. She's afraid it might have changed.

title: The Cab Driver's Passenger
excerpt: Vikram had been driving cabs for eleven years. He thought he had seen everything. He had not seen this.
content: Vikram had been driving cabs for eleven years. He thought he had seen everything — the crying executive, the drunk newlyweds, the man who asked to be driven in circles for an hour without a word of explanation. He had not seen this.

The pickup was from a residential colony at 2 AM. No name, just a pin drop and a phone number that went unanswered when he tried to confirm. He almost cancelled. Then the passenger appeared at the gate.
Young woman, early twenties, dark clothing, no bag. She got into the back seat and gave him an address in the old part of the city — a lane he vaguely recognized but couldn't quite place.

She didn't speak. He turned on the radio to fill the silence. The station played static. He turned it off. The GPS showed her destination was forty minutes away, then ten minutes into the drive, it showed five minutes. Then it refreshed and showed forty again.
He glanced in the mirror. She was staring out the window. Her breath didn't fog the glass.
It was December.
He focused on the road. When he pulled onto a main boulevard, he checked the mirror again. The backseat was empty.

He stopped the car. Got out. Looked in every direction. There was no one. No side streets she could have slipped onto, no shops open, no other cars. He had not stopped moving. He had not slowed below 40 km/h.
He sat back in the driver's seat, hands trembling, and looked at the Ola app. The ride was still active. The timer was still running. The passenger was marked as 'in vehicle'.
The destination updated on its own. The address changed to a location just three minutes away — a hospital. He looked it up. The hospital had been demolished in 2008.
He cancelled the ride and drove home.
Two days later, a friend sent him a news link. A girl had been reported missing from that colony, her family desperately looking for her. The photo was of the girl he'd picked up.
She had been missing since the previous December. One year before his ride.

title: The Mirror That Showed Tomorrow
excerpt: The antique mirror was beautiful and cheap, which should have been the first warning. The second warning came three days after they hung it.
content: The antique mirror was beautiful and cheap. Arjun found it at a roadside stall near the old bazar, the frame carved with such intricate detail that he assumed it must be a reproduction. Still, at eight hundred rupees, he didn't ask questions.

His wife Deepa hung it in the bedroom hallway. It made the narrow passage feel wider, and the slight tarnish at its edges gave their new flat a feeling of age and character. They liked it. For three days they liked it very much.
On the fourth morning, Deepa stopped in front of it on her way to brush her teeth and did not move for a long time.
Arjun came out and found her standing there. He assumed she was checking her hair. Then he looked at the mirror.

The reflection showed the hallway correctly — the same walls, the same light, the same coat hooks. Deepa was reflected in it accurately. But she was wearing different clothes. A blue salwar she had bought last month and hadn't unpacked yet.
In the mirror, Arjun wasn't there.
They laughed about it nervously. A trick of the light, a distortion. Old mirrors do strange things. They went to work and didn't talk about it.
That evening, Deepa wore the blue salwar. She hadn't planned to. She opened the cupboard looking for something else and it was the first thing she pulled out.
She stopped. Remembered the morning. Put the salwar back and wore something different, deliberately, shaking.

She watched the mirror closely for two weeks. It always showed her the next morning, twelve hours ahead, accurate in every detail she could verify. What she was wearing. Whether her hair was up or down. Whether she was smiling.
She stopped watching the mirror the morning it showed her crying, alone in the hallway, hands pressed against the wall.
She removed it that same day. She did not tell Arjun why.
Two weeks later, Arjun asked her for a divorce.

title: The Review That Wrote Itself
excerpt: The restaurant had no address. It had no website. It had 1,400 five-star reviews on Google Maps, and the most recent one was posted fourteen minutes ago.
content: The restaurant had no address. It had no website, no phone number, no owner listed anywhere. It had 1,400 five-star reviews on Google Maps, and the most recent one was posted fourteen minutes ago.

Karan found it while looking for somewhere to eat near his hotel on a work trip. The pin on the map floated over a block of old commercial buildings, but the little orange dot on street view showed only a shutter, padlocked shut.

He scrolled through the reviews anyway. They were incredibly specific — reviewers describing dishes, flavors, the exact chair they had sat in, the way the light came through the window. One reviewer said the daal was 'the closest thing to what my grandmother made, and she has been gone for twenty years.' Another said the owner knew their name before they gave it.
The reviews were from accounts with full histories, photos, other reviews of ordinary places. Real people.
Karan posted a question in the reviews section: 'How do you get a reservation?' He put his phone away and went to bed.
At midnight, he had a reply. Not a public reply — a Google Maps direct message, from the restaurant's owner account. It said: 'You don't. You are already on the list. We will see you at 8.'
He hadn't given his hotel address. He hadn't mentioned his city. His Google profile listed his home city, four hours away.
At 7:58 PM the next evening, Karan stood in front of his hotel wondering why he had put on his best shirt. He looked at the padlocked shutter in his maps app. Then he walked there anyway.
The shutter was open. A light was on inside.

He has never written a review about what happened next. He has never talked about it publicly. But his friends say he is different now — calmer, like a man who has been told something important and decided to believe it.
If you search for the restaurant today, the listing is still there. The review count is now 1,401.
The most recent review, posted last night, was written by Karan.

title: The Employee Who Came Back
excerpt: The resignation email came at 11:58 PM. The strange part wasn't the timing. It was that Meera had died four months earlier.
content: The resignation email came at 11:58 PM on a Tuesday. Preethi, the HR manager, almost didn't see it until the next morning. When she did, she read it three times before calling her manager.
The email was from Meera Iyer. Politely worded. Thanking the company for four good years. Requesting that her final month's salary be processed. Signed with her usual closing — 'Warm regards, Meera.'
Meera Iyer had died four months earlier. A road accident. Preethi had attended the funeral herself.

The email had come from Meera's official company ID — the same one that had been deactivated the week after her death. IT confirmed the account showed no login activity since deactivation. No one else had access credentials. The email had simply appeared in the outbox at 11:58 PM, timestamp verified by the mail server.
Preethi called Meera's mother, carefully, without explaining why. She asked if Meera had kept a second phone, or if anyone had access to her old work laptop.
Meera's mother was quiet for a long moment. Then she said: 'She told me she had one last thing to sort out at work. Before she— she always worried about leaving things unfinished.'
This was a conversation Meera's mother said she'd had with her daughter.
Two weeks before the accident.
The salary was processed. It felt wrong not to. The amount sat in Meera's bank account, which remained open because no one had filed the paperwork yet. Preethi checked once, three months later.
The money had been transferred out. To a children's shelter Meera had volunteered at for years.
The transfer note read: 'One last thing.'

title: The Flat That Wasn't Listed
excerpt: The listing disappeared the day Tanvi moved in. The broker had no record of ever showing it. The landlord said the flat had been empty for three years.
content: Tanvi found the flat on a housing app while desperately searching for a place near her new office. Two-bedroom, furnished, rent that seemed too good, available immediately. She called the number in the listing and a broker answered, cheerful and efficient.

He showed her the flat the same evening. It was perfect — clean, quiet, a slight smell of old wood and incense, a balcony that looked over a tree-lined lane. She paid the deposit the next morning and moved in over the weekend.

On Monday, she tried to find the original listing to share with a friend. It wasn't there. Not archived, not expired — simply gone, as though it had never been posted. The broker's number now went to a different person entirely, a man who said he'd had the number for six years and had never done any brokerage work.
Tanvi asked her landlord about it. He was an elderly man who lived on the ground floor. He looked confused when she mentioned the listing and the broker.
'I didn't list it,' he said. 'I hadn't decided to rent it. The flat has been empty since my wife passed. I was going to let it stay that way.'
He had no explanation for how she'd gotten a key, signed a lease, or paid a deposit — documents he said he hadn't signed and money he said he hadn't received.
Yet there she was. In the flat. With a key that worked.

She stayed. The rent was being deducted from her account each month, going somewhere she couldn't trace. The flat was quiet. The neighbors were kind. The elderly landlord brought her tea sometimes and called her by his wife's name when he forgot himself.
She found a photograph in a drawer she hadn't opened yet. A woman standing on the balcony, looking out at the lane.
She recognized the balcony. She recognized the angle. She'd taken almost the same photo herself, her first evening there.
The woman in the photograph was wearing Tanvi's coat. The coat Tanvi had bought three weeks ago.

title: The Intern Who Knew Too Much
excerpt: She answered questions about projects that hadn't started yet. She mentioned people by name before she'd met them. Everyone assumed she'd just done her research.
content: Kavya joined the team in June, fresh out of college, quieter than most interns. She sat at the desk near the window and took notes in a small paper notebook nobody had seen anyone use in years.

The first strange moment came in her second week. In a meeting about a new product roadmap, she mentioned a technical constraint that hadn't been discussed yet — one the engineering lead was about to raise. He assumed she'd read the pre-read document. There hadn't been a pre-read document.
She referred to the head of marketing, Suresh, by name on her first day — before the introductions. She said she'd heard it somewhere. Nobody remembered saying it near her.

In July, she told a colleague not to take the stairs near the east wing because 'they're going to fix them today.' The maintenance crew arrived that afternoon for an unscheduled inspection. The order had been placed that morning, after Kavya spoke.

People started paying attention. She never made predictions in a way that sounded like prediction. She spoke in the past tense, as if recalling. 'I think the client came back to us on that' — and the email would arrive within the hour.
HR called her in to ask how she was settling in. She said she liked the team, that it felt familiar. She asked how long the woman before her had lasted in the role.
There had been no one before her. The role was newly created.
She looked briefly confused. Then she smiled and said she must have misread something in the onboarding.
Her internship ended in August. She left a note on her desk that said only: 'See you next time.' Below it, a list of names — twelve people on the team — with small notations next to each.
Accurate notations. Things that hadn't happened yet.
The last name on the list was her own manager's. The notation next to it was a date.
He left the company on that date. He hasn't told anyone why.

title: The Train Seat That Was Already Taken
excerpt: His seat was occupied. The passenger in it had his name on a ticket with the same PNR. Same train. Same date. Same berth.
content: Ashwin boarded the Rajdhani with ten minutes to spare, found his coach, found his berth, and found someone already sitting in it. This happens. He politely showed his ticket. The man in the seat showed him his.
Same PNR number. Same train. Same date. Same berth number.
The TTE was called. He scanned both tickets. Both came back valid. He checked his records. Only one ticket had been issued against that PNR. He looked between the two men.
The other passenger was quiet throughout. He hadn't argued, hadn't raised his voice, hadn't offered an explanation. He simply held his ticket and waited.
The TTE, flustered, asked the man for his name. He gave one. The TTE asked for ID. The man reached into his jacket, and then stopped. He looked at Ashwin directly for the first time.
'You don't remember me,' he said. It wasn't a question.
Ashwin didn't. He had never seen this man in his life.

The man stood, folded his ticket carefully, and walked toward the door of the coach. The TTE called after him. He didn't stop. By the time the TTE reached the door, the coach beyond was empty. The train was moving. There was nowhere to go.
Ashwin used the berth. He didn't sleep.
At the destination the next morning, he checked his pockets for his wallet and found a folded piece of paper he hadn't put there. It was the other man's ticket.
He looked up the PNR later out of curiosity. The ticket had been booked eighteen years ago.
For a passenger who, according to the records, had never completed that journey.

title: The Doctor's Second Opinion
excerpt: The scan showed a shadow they couldn't explain. The second doctor said the shadow had already been explained — by a patient who died in that room six years ago.
content: Rajan went in for a routine chest scan. He wasn't worried. He had no symptoms, no family history, no reason to be there except his company insurance required an annual check.

The radiologist called him back the same afternoon. There was a shadow on the scan. Not a mass, not a lesion — a shadow. Distinct, unusual, sitting at the edge of the left lung like something resting there.

She referred him to a specialist. The specialist looked at the scan for a long time without speaking. Then he asked if Rajan had ever been in an accident, or had any prior chest surgery. Rajan hadn't.
The specialist asked which machine had produced the scan. He was told the room number. He went quiet again.
He referred Rajan to a colleague — not for a second scan, he said, but because his colleague had 'context' he didn't.

The colleague was a senior radiologist who had worked at the hospital for decades. She looked at the scan. Then she opened a filing cabinet and pulled out a folder. Inside was a scan from six years ago, belonging to a patient who had died in the room where Rajan's scan was taken — a man who had insisted, until the end, that something was resting in his chest that didn't belong to him.
The shadows on both scans were identical. Same shape. Same position. Same faint density.
Rajan's follow-up scan, taken on a different machine in a different room, showed nothing.
The shadow was gone.
He asked the senior radiologist what she thought it meant. She said she had stopped trying to explain it after the third time she'd seen it happen in that room.
'Third?' he asked.
She put the folder away and didn't answer.

title: The Password Nobody Set
excerpt: The family photo album had been set to private with a password. No one in the family had done it. The password, when they finally guessed it, was a date — a date that hadn't happened yet.
content: Sunita created the shared family album online in 2019, the year her parents moved cities and everyone wanted to stay connected. She added her parents, her brother in Pune, and her cousin in the UK. It was meant to be simple — a place to drop photos without messaging them one by one.

In early 2022, her brother tried to access the album to add pictures from a wedding and found it locked. Password protected. He messaged the family group. Nobody had done it. Sunita tried her admin credentials. They didn't work. The account listed the last change as having been made by 'account owner' — which was Sunita — at 3:04 AM on a night she had been asleep in a different city.

They contacted support. Support confirmed the change had been made from a device they didn't recognize, at an IP address that resolved to a location none of them had ever visited — a small town in Rajasthan no family member had any connection to.
They tried to guess the password. Her parents' anniversary. Her brother's birthday. Her own name in various forms. Childhood nicknames. The street they grew up on.
Her father, half joking, typed in a date. The album opened.
Nobody asked him why he'd tried that particular date. His expression was strange enough that they didn't want to.
He died eleven months later. Unexpectedly, without warning, on the date he had typed.

The album is still accessible. The photos are still there — birthdays, holidays, ordinary afternoons. Sunita hasn't touched it in two years. She's afraid that if she checks the password change logs again, she'll find another date she doesn't recognize.
One that hasn't passed yet.
